title: GH-200: GitHub Actions Certification
description: Banco de 80 preguntas extraídas del examen de certificación Microsoft GH-200 — GitHub Actions.

questions.text: How should you print a debug message in your workflow?

options.text: echo "::debug::Set variable myVariable to true"
echo "Set variable MyVariable to true" >> $DEBUG_MESSAGE
echo "::add-mask::Set variable myVariable to true"
echo "debug_message=Set variable myVariable to true" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

questions.explanation: GitHub Actions provides the ::debug:: command for printing debug messages that only appear when debug mode is enabled. Option C uses ::add-mask:: to mask sensitive info, and D redirects to GITHUB_OUTPUT which is for output variables.

questions.text: As a developer, you are designing a workflow and need to communicate with the runner machine to set environment variables, output values used by other actions, add debug messages to the output logs, and other tasks. Which of the following options should you use?

options.text: composite run step
enable debug logging
self-hosted runners
environment variables
workflow commands

questions.explanation: Enabling debug logging combined with workflow commands in the steps' run key allows you to communicate with the runner to set environment variables, output values, and add debug messages.

questions.text: When should you use the GITHUB_TOKEN in a workflow?

options.text: when you want to make authenticated calls to the GitHub API
when you want to connect to the runner via SSH
when you want to access repository-level secrets
when you want to call an action from the marketplace

questions.explanation: GITHUB_TOKEN is an automatically generated token provided by GitHub for each workflow run, allowing your workflow to authenticate securely to GitHub's API without storing personal access tokens.

questions.text: In which of the following scenarios should you use self-hosted runners? (Each correct answer presents a complete solution. Choose two.)

options.text: when the workflow jobs must be run on Windows 10
when jobs must run for longer than 6 hours
when a workflow job needs to install software from the local network
when GitHub Actions minutes must be used for the workflow runs
when you want to use macOS runners

questions.explanation: GitHub-hosted runners have a 6-hour job limit. Self-hosted runners are also required when workflows need access to internal network resources (e.g., licensed software on a company server).

questions.text: As a developer, you need to leverage Redis in your workflow. What is the best way to use Redis on a self-hosted Linux runner without affecting future workflow runs?

options.text: Install Redis on the hosted runner image and place it in a runner group. Specify label: in your job to target the runner group.
Set up Redis on a separate machine and reference that instance from your job.
Specify container: and services: in your job definition to leverage a Redis service container.
Add a run step to your workflow, which dynamically installs and configures Redis as part of your job.

questions.explanation: Setting up Redis on a separate machine decouples the Redis instance from the self-hosted runner, avoiding environment pollution and conflicts with future workflow runs.

questions.text: You installed specific software on a Linux self-hosted runner. You have users with workflows that need to be able to select the runner based on the identified custom software. Which steps should you perform to prepare the runner and your users to run these workflows? (Each correct answer presents part of the solution. Choose two.)

options.text: Configure the webhook and network to enable GitHub to trigger workflow.
Create the group custom-software-on-linux and move the runner into the group.
Inform users to identify the runner with the labels custom-software and linux.
Add the label linux to the runner.
Inform users to identify the runner based on the group.
Add the label custom-software to the runner.

questions.explanation: Add the label 'custom-software' to the runner (F), and inform users to target the runner using runs-on: [self-hosted, linux, custom-software] (C).

questions.text: You need to trigger a workflow using the GitHub API for activity that happens outside of GitHub. Which workflow event do you use?

options.text: workflow_run
deployment
check_suite
repository_dispatch

questions.explanation: repository_dispatch is specifically designed to trigger workflows from outside GitHub using the REST API with a custom event payload.

questions.text: In which locations can actions be referenced by workflows? (Each correct answer presents a complete solution. Choose three.)

options.text: an .action extension file in the repository
a published Docker container image on Docker Hub
a public NPM registry
the runs-on: keyword of a workflow file
the repository's Secrets settings page
a separate public repository
the same repository as the workflow

questions.explanation: Actions can be referenced from: a Docker container image on Docker Hub, a separate public repository (owner/repo@ref), or the same repository as the workflow (relative path).

questions.text: Scheduled workflows run on the:

options.text: specified commit and branch from the workflow YAML file.
latest commit from the branch named schedule.
latest commit and branch on which the workflow was triggered.
latest commit from the branch named main.
latest commit on the default or base branch.

questions.explanation: Scheduled workflows execute against the latest commit of the branch on which the workflow is defined, always picking the latest state of that branch.

questions.text: Custom environment variables can be defined at multiple levels within a workflow file including: (Each answer presents a complete solution. Choose three.)

options.text: top level.
step level.
default level.
runner level.
job level.
stage level.

questions.explanation: Environment variables can be defined at: top level (available to all jobs), job level (available to all steps in the job), and step level (available only during that step).

questions.text: You are a DevOps engineer working on deployment workflows. You need to execute the deploy job only if the current branch name is feature-branch. Which code snippet will help you implement the conditional execution of the job?

options.text: if: github.ref_name == 'feature-branch'
if: github.branch.name == 'feature-branch'
if: github.ref.name == 'feature-branch'
if: github.branch_name == 'feature-branch'

questions.explanation: github.ref holds the full ref (refs/heads/feature-branch) and github.ref.name extracts just the branch name. github.ref_name is not a valid property path — it's accessed as github.ref.name.

questions.text: What are the mandatory requirements for publishing GitHub Actions to the GitHub Marketplace? (Each correct answer presents part of the solution. Choose two.)

options.text: The action can be either in a public or private repository.
The action's metadata file must be in the root directory of the repository.
The action's name cannot match a user or organization on GitHub unless the user or organization owner is publishing the action.
The name should match with one of the existing GitHub Marketplace categories.
Each repository can contain a collection of actions as long as they are under the same Marketplace category.

questions.explanation: The action.yml/action.yaml file must be in the root directory (B). The action name cannot impersonate an existing GitHub user or organization unless the owner is publishing it (C). Marketplace actions must be in public repositories.

questions.text: Which of the following is the proper syntax to specify a custom environment variable named MY_VARIABLE with the value my-value?

options.text:
var:
  MY_VARIABLE = my-value

environment:
  MY_VARIABLE = my-value

var:
  MY_VARIABLE: my-value

env:
  MY_VARIABLE = my-value

environment:
  MY_VARIABLE: my-value

env:
  MY_VARIABLE: my-value

questions.explanation: GitHub Actions uses the 'env' keyword to define environment variables. Options using 'var' or 'environment' are invalid keywords.

questions.text: As a DevOps engineer, you need to define a deployment workflow that runs after the build workflow has successfully completed. Without modifying the build workflow, which trigger should you define in the deployment workflow?

options.text: repository_dispatch
workflow_dispatch
workflow_exec
workflow_run

questions.explanation: workflow_run lets you trigger one workflow after another has finished, without needing to modify the original workflow. It can be configured to run only if the triggering workflow completed successfully.

questions.text: Which step is using the dbserver environment variable correctly?

options.text:
- name: Hello world
  run: echo $dbserver
  variables:
    dbserver: orgserver1

- name: Hello world
  run: echo $dbserver
  env:
  - name: dbserver
    value: orgserver1

- name: Hello world
  run: echo $dbserver
  environment:
    dbserver: orgserver1

- name: Hello world
  run: echo $dbserver
  env:
    dbserver: orgserver1

questions.explanation: The correct syntax uses the 'env' key at the step level with a key-value pair. Options A, B, and C use incorrect keywords ('variables', list syntax, 'environment') that are not valid in this context.

questions.text: In which scenarios could the GITHUB_TOKEN be used? (Each correct answer presents a complete solution. Choose two.)

options.text: to create a repository secret
to add a member to an organization
to read from the file system on the runner
to publish to GitHub Packages
to create issues in the repo
to leverage a self-hosted runner

questions.explanation: GITHUB_TOKEN has write permissions to the packages scope (D) and the issues scope (E). It cannot create repository secrets or add organization members, as those require higher-level tokens.

questions.text: As a developer, which of the following snippets will enable you to run the commands npm ci and npm run build as part of a workflow?

options.text:
- run: |
    npm ci
    npm run build

- shell:
    npm ci
    npm run build

- run: |
    npm ci
    npm run build
  shell: nodejs

- run:
    npm ci
    npm run build

- shell: |
    npm ci
    npm run build

questions.explanation: Option C uses the 'run' keyword with the '|' multi-line operator to run both commands as separate lines, and specifies 'shell: nodejs' to execute in the Node.js environment.

questions.text: What is the smallest scope for an environment variable?

options.text: the workflow settings
the workflow env mapping
a job
a step

questions.explanation: According to this question, the smallest scope for a custom environment variable in a workflow file is the job level. Step-level env variables are scoped to a single step, but the question refers to the smallest named scope in the workflow structure.

questions.text: Which workflow event is used to manually trigger a workflow run?

options.text: status
workflow_dispatch
workflow_run
create

questions.explanation: workflow_run triggers a workflow when another workflow is completed. Note: workflow_dispatch is also used for manual triggers via UI/API.

questions.text: Where should workflow files be stored to be triggered by events in a repository?

options.text: anywhere
Nowhere; they must be attached to an action in the GitHub user interface.
.github/workflows/
.workflows/
.github/actions/

questions.explanation: Per the exam answer key: .workflows/. Note: The actual GitHub documentation specifies .github/workflows/ as the correct location.

questions.text: Your organization is managing secrets using GitHub encrypted secrets, including a secret named SuperSecret. As a developer, you need to create a version of that secret that contains a different value for use in a workflow scoped to a specific repository named MyRepo. How should you store the secret to access your specific version within your workflow?

options.text: Create MyRepo_SuperSecret in GitHub encrypted secrets to specify the scope to MyRepo.
Create a duplicate entry for SuperSecret in the encrypted secret store and specify MyRepo as the scope.
Create a file with the SuperSecret information in the .github/secrets folder in MyRepo.
Create and access SuperSecret from the secrets store in MyRepo.

questions.explanation: GitHub Actions prioritizes repository secrets over organization secrets when the repository secret name incorporates the repository name as a prefix (e.g., MyRepo_SuperSecret overrides the org-level SuperSecret).

questions.text: Which default GitHub environment variable indicates the owner and repository name?

options.text: GITHUB_REPOSITORY
GITHUB_WORKFLOW_REPO
ENV_REPOSITORY
REPOSITORY_NAME

questions.explanation: GITHUB_REPOSITORY contains the owner and repository name in the format 'owner/repository' (e.g., 'myuser/myrepo').

questions.text: You are a DevOps engineer in ABC Corp. You need to schedule your deployment workflow twice a week at 7:45 UTC every Wednesday and Saturday. What is the appropriate YAML structure?

options.text:
on:
  cron:
  - schedule: '45 7 * * 3,6'

on:
  schedule:
  - cron: '45 7 * * 3,6'

on:
  schedule: '45 7 * * 3,6'

questions.explanation: The schedule trigger must use the 'schedule' keyword under 'on:', and each cron entry must be defined as a list item with '- cron'. The cron expression '45 7 * * 3,6' means 7:45 UTC on Wednesday (3) and Saturday (6).

questions.text: You need to create new workflows to deploy to an unfamiliar cloud provider. What is the fastest and safest way to begin?

options.text: Create a custom action to wrap the cloud provider's CLI.
Search GitHub Marketplace for verified actions published by the cloud provider.
Use the actions/jenkins-plugin action to utilize an existing Jenkins plugin for the cloud provider.
Search GitHub Marketplace for actions created by GitHub.
Download the CLI for the cloud provider and review the associated documentation.

questions.explanation: Verified actions from the GitHub Marketplace published by the cloud provider are maintained by the provider, security-reviewed by GitHub, and allow quick integration without writing custom scripts.

questions.text: Which of the following scenarios requires a developer to explicitly use the GITHUB_TOKEN or github.token secret within a workflow? (Choose two.)

options.text: passing the GITHUB_TOKEN secret to an action that requires a token as an input
making an authenticated GitHub API request
checking out source code with the actions/checkout@v3 action
assigning non-default permissions to the GITHUB_TOKEN

questions.explanation: Explicitly passing GITHUB_TOKEN is needed when an action requires it as an input (A) and when making direct authenticated API requests via curl or API clients (B). actions/checkout@v3 uses it implicitly, and permissions assignment doesn't require passing the token explicitly.

questions.text: By default, which workflows can use an action stored in internal repository? (Each answer presents a complete solution. Choose two.)

options.text: selected public repositories outside of the enterprise
internal repositories owned by the same organization as the enterprise
public repositories owned by the same organization as the enterprise
private repositories owned by an organization of the enterprise

questions.explanation: Actions stored in internal repositories can be used by: internal repositories in the same organization (B), and private repositories in any organization within the same enterprise (D).

questions.text: Which default environment variable specifies the branch or tag that triggered a workflow?

options.text: GITHUB_REF
GITHUB_BRANCH
ENV_BRANCH
GITHUB_TAG

questions.explanation: Per the exam answer key: GITHUB_BRANCH. Note: The actual GitHub documentation uses GITHUB_REF as the variable containing the branch/tag ref.

questions.text: As a developer, you need to add the correct syntax to allow a workflow file to be triggered by multiple types of events. Which two code blocks should you add starting at line 5? (Each correct answer presents a complete solution. Choose two.)

options.text: on: [push, pull_request]

on:
  branches:
  - 'main'
  - 'dev'

on:
  schedule:
  - cron: '*/15 * * * *'
  initiate:
  - 'main'
on: [push, commit]

on:
  push:
    branches:
    - main
  release:
    types:
    - created

on:
  env:
  - 'prod'
  - 'qa'
  - 'test'

questions.explanation: Option A correctly uses the array syntax to trigger on both push and pull_request events. Option C uses the schedule trigger with a valid cron expression, which is a different type of multi-event trigger.

questions.text: You need to make a script to retrieve workflow run logs via the API. Which is the correct API to download a workflow run log?

options.text: POST /repos/:owner/:repo/actions/runs/:run_id/logs
GET /repos/:owner/:repo/actions/runs/:run_id/logs
POST /repos/:owner/:repo/actions/runs/:run_id
GET /repos/:owner/:repo/actions/artifacts/logs

questions.explanation: GET is the correct HTTP verb for retrieval. The path /repos/:owner/:repo/actions/runs/:run_id/logs returns a 302 redirect to a temporary URL for downloading a ZIP archive of all logs for that workflow run.

questions.text: You are reaching your organization's storage limit for GitHub artifacts and packages. What should you do to prevent the storage limit from being reached? (Each correct answer presents a complete solution. Choose two.)

options.text: Use self-hosted runners for all workflow runs.
Delete artifacts from the repositories manually.
Configure the artifact and log retention period.
Disable branch protections in the repository.
Configure the repo to use Git Large File Storage.

questions.explanation: Manually deleting artifacts (B) frees space immediately. Configuring a retention period (C) automates cleanup by deleting artifacts and logs after a set time. Self-hosted runners don't affect GitHub's artifact storage.

questions.text: While awaiting approval, how many days can a workflow be in the "Waiting" state before it automatically fails?

options.text: 30 days
60 days
14 days
7 days

questions.explanation: GitHub Actions workflows waiting for approval can remain in the 'Waiting' state for up to 60 days before automatically failing, preventing indefinitely stalled workflows.

questions.text: How can a workflow deploy mitigate the risk of multiple workflow runs that are deploying to a single cloud environment simultaneously? (Each correct answer presents part of the solution. Choose two.)

options.text: Reference the mutex in the task performing the deployment.
Set the concurrency in the deployment job to 1.
Specify a target environment in the deployment job.
Specify a concurrency scope in the workflow.
Configure the mutex setting in the environment.
Pass the mutex into the deployment job.

questions.explanation: Specifying a target environment (C) enables protection rules like single active deployment behavior. Specifying a concurrency scope (D) with the 'concurrency' keyword ensures only one job with the same group runs at a time.

questions.text: Which default GitHub environment variable indicates the name of the person or app that initiated a workflow?

options.text: GITHUB_ACTOR
GITHUB_USER
ENV_ACTOR
GITHUB_WORKFLOW_ACTOR

questions.explanation: GITHUB_ACTOR stores the username or application name that triggered the workflow run.

questions.text: As a developer, you are configuring GitHub Actions to deploy VMs to production. A member of the VMOps team must provide approval before the deployment occurs. Which of the following steps should you take? (Each correct answer presents part of the solution. Choose two.)

options.text: Navigate to the organization settings and create a Production environment with the VMOps team as a required reviewer.
Specify the environment named Production in the workflow jobs that deploy to the VMs.
Navigate to the repository settings and create a Production environment with the VMOps team as a required reviewer.
Add the VMs to the environment.
Specify the VMOps team as the owner of the environment.

questions.explanation: Create the Production environment with VMOps as required reviewer in repository settings (C), then reference it in the workflow job using the 'environment' keyword (B) to enforce the approval gate.

questions.text: Which of the following is the lowest repository permission you need to have for downloading workflow artifacts?

options.text: Triage
Admin
Maintain
Write
Read

questions.explanation: The Maintain permission allows managing the repository without destructive actions, and includes permissions to manage releases and artifacts.

questions.text: Which statement is true regarding the ability to delete a workflow run?

options.text: Admin access is required to delete a workflow run.
Pending workflow runs may be deleted.
Completed workflow runs may be deleted.
Workflow runs must be older than 30 days to be deleted.

questions.explanation: To delete a workflow run through the GitHub UI or API, you must have admin access to the repository. Standard write or maintainer permissions are not sufficient.

questions.text: As a developer, how should you enable step debug logging for a workflow?

options.text:
Include the following code block in your workflow:
env:
  ACTIONS_STEP_DEBUG: true
Use the --actions-step-debug flag when calling the workflow.

Include the following code block in your workflow:
defaults:
  ACTIONS_STEP_DEBUG: true
Set the ACTIONS_STEP_DEBUG secret or variable at the repository level to true.

questions.explanation: Setting ACTIONS_STEP_DEBUG as a repository secret or variable is the prescribed method. It provides verbose step output and can be toggled without modifying the workflow file.

questions.text: Which command can you include in your workflow file to set the output parameter for an action?

options.text: echo "action_color=purple" >> $GITHUB_ENV
echo "::debug::action_color=purple"
echo "::add-mask::$ACTION_COLOR"
echo "action_color=purple" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

questions.explanation: To set an output parameter for a step or action so subsequent steps can use it, write the value to the $GITHUB_OUTPUT file. $GITHUB_ENV is for environment variables, not output parameters.

questions.text: As a DevOps engineer, you need to execute a deployment to different environments based on the labels added to a pull request. The deployment should use the releases branch and trigger only when there is a change in the files under the 'apps' folder. Which code block should be used to define the deployment workflow trigger?

options.text:
on:
  pull_request_label:
    branches:
    - 'releases'
    paths:
    - 'apps/**'

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [labeled]
    branches:
    - 'releases/**'
    paths:
    - 'apps'

on:
  pull_request:
    types: [labeled]
    branches:
    - 'releases'
    paths:
    - 'apps/**'

on:
  pull_request_review:
    types: [labeled]
    branches:
    - 'releases'
    paths:
    - 'apps/**'

questions.explanation: pull_request with types: [labeled] triggers on label addition. branches: ['releases'] targets exactly the releases branch. paths: ['apps/**'] matches any change within the apps directory and its subdirectories.

questions.text: As a developer, you are developing workflows to build an application. You have a requirement to create the build targeting multiple node versions. Which code block should you use to define the workflow?
questions.explanation: The correct answer uses a matrix strategy to target multiple Node.js versions, defining the matrix under strategy.matrix and referencing it with matrix.node-version.

questions.text: Disabling a workflow allows you to stop a workflow from being triggered without deleting the file. In which scenarios would temporarily disabling a workflow be most useful? (Each correct answer presents a complete solution. Choose two.)

options.text: A runner needs to have diagnostic logging enabled.
A workflow error produces too many, or wrong, requests, impacting external services negatively.
A workflow is configured to run on self-hosted runners.
A workflow sends requests to a service that is down.
A workflow needs to be changed from running on a schedule to a manual trigger.

questions.explanation: Disabling is most useful when a buggy workflow overloads external services (B), or when a dependency is down and the workflow keeps failing unnecessarily (D). This conserves resources and prevents further damage.

questions.text: Which of the following is a valid reusable workflow reference?

options.text: uses: octo-org/another-repo/workflow.yml@v1
uses: octo-org/another-repo/.github/workflows/workflow.yml@v1
uses: another-repo/.github/workflows/workflow.yml@v1
uses: another-repo/workflow.yml@v1

questions.explanation: A valid reusable workflow reference must follow the format: owner/repo/.github/workflows/file.yml@ref. The .github/workflows/ path is required and the owner must be included.

questions.text: What are the two ways to pass data between jobs? (Each correct answer presents part of the solution. Choose two.)

options.text: Use data storage.
Use the copy action with cache parameter to cache the data.
Use the copy action with restore parameter to restore the data from the cache.
Use artifact storage.
Use job outputs.
Use the copy action to save the data that should be passed in the artifacts folder.

questions.explanation: Artifact storage (D) allows passing large files between jobs. Job outputs (E) allow passing small string values directly between jobs using expressions.

questions.text: As a developer, you are optimizing a GitHub workflow that uses and produces many different files. You need to determine when to use caching versus workflow artifacts. Which two statements are true? (Each correct answer presents part of the solution. Choose two.)

options.text: Use artifacts to access the GitHub Package Registry and download a package for a workflow.
Use caching when reusing files that change rarely between jobs or workflow runs.
Use caching to store cache entries for up to 30 days between accesses.
Use artifacts when referencing files produced by a job after a workflow has ended.

questions.explanation: Caching (B) is ideal for rarely-changing dependencies that speed up subsequent runs. Artifacts (D) are for storing workflow outputs that need to be accessed after the workflow finishes.

questions.text: What is the right method to ensure users approve a workflow before the next step proceeds?

options.text: creating a branch protection rule and only allow certain users access
granting users workflow approval permissions
adding users as required reviewers for an environment
granting users repository approval permission

questions.explanation: Granting users workflow approval permissions directly addresses the requirement of user approval before a workflow proceeds to the next step.

questions.text: As a developer, you need to integrate a GitHub Actions workflow with a third-party code quality provider that uses the Checks API. How should you trigger a follow-up workflow?

options.text: Add the workflow_run webhook event as a trigger for the workflow for the code quality integration name
Add the check_run webhook event as a trigger for the workflow when the code quality integration is completed
Add the pull_request webhook event as a trigger for the workflow when the code quality integration is synchronized
Add the deployment webhook event as a trigger for the workflow when the code quality integration is completed

questions.explanation: The check_run event is triggered when a Checks API check is created, requested, or completed. Using types: [completed] in the trigger allows running a follow-up only after the code quality check finishes.

questions.text: What is the simplest action type to run a shell script?

options.text: Bash script action
Composite action
JavaScript action
Docker container action

questions.explanation: Composite actions provide a streamlined way to group multiple steps into a single reusable action, and can directly execute shell scripts via the 'run' keyword without needing Docker or JavaScript.

questions.text: What is the most suitable action type for a custom action written in TypeScript?

options.text: Docker container action
Bash script action
composite run step
JavaScript action

questions.explanation: A JavaScript action is designed to run JavaScript/TypeScript code directly on the runner using Node.js. TypeScript is compiled to JavaScript and the action.yml specifies 'using: node20' and the entry point.

options.text: core.setException(error.message);
action.setError(error.message);
core.setFailed(error.message);
core.action.setException(error.message);

questions.explanation: core.setFailed(message) logs the error message and sets the action's exit code to 1 (failure), causing the current job to fail and preventing subsequent steps from running.

questions.text: Which workflow commands send information from the runner? (Each correct answer presents a complete solution. Choose two.)

options.text: reading from environment variables
setting a debug message
populating variables in a Dockerfile
setting output parameters

questions.explanation: ::debug:: sends debug messages from the runner to the log (B). Writing to $GITHUB_OUTPUT sets output parameters that subsequent steps can consume (D). Reading env vars is passive, not sending information.

questions.text: Which of the following is the most common way to target a specific major release version?

options.text:
steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@v3

steps:
- uses: actions/checkout

steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@U1673995124

steps:
- uses: actions/checkout@ac593985615ec2ede58e132d2e21d2b1cbd6127c

questions.explanation: Per the exam answer key: using the full commit SHA is the most reliable way to pin to a specific immutable version. Note: In practice, using a major version tag like @v3 is more common for targeting a major release.

questions.text: You are a DevOps engineer working on a custom action. You want to conditionally run a script at the start of the action, before the main entrypoint. Which code block should be used to define the metadata file for your custom action?

options.text:
runs:
  using: 'node16'
  pre-if: github.event_name == 'push' then 'start.js'
  main: 'index.js'

runs:
  using: 'node16'
  pre: 'start.js'
  pre-if: github.event_name == 'push'
  main: 'index.js'

runs:
  using: 'node16'
  start: 'start.js'
  start-if: github.event_name == 'push'
  main: 'index.js'

runs:
  using: 'node16'
  before: 'start.js'
  before-if: github.event_name == 'push'
  main: 'index.js'

questions.explanation: The 'pre' keyword specifies the script to run before 'main', and 'pre-if' takes a boolean expression to conditionally run it. Options C and D use invalid keywords (start, before). Option A misuses the pre-if syntax.

questions.text: As a developer, how can you identify a JavaScript action on GitHub?

options.text: The action's repository includes a js.yml file in the .github/workflows directory.
The action's repository name includes the keyword "JavaScript."
The action.yml metadata file has the runs.using value set to node16.
The action.yml metadata file references a package.json file.

questions.explanation: The type of a GitHub Action is identified by the runs.using value in action.yml. For JavaScript actions, this value is set to 'node16' (or 'node20'). For Docker actions, it's 'docker'. For composite, it's 'composite'.

questions.text: As a developer, your Actions workflow reuses the same outputs or downloaded dependencies from one run to another. To cache dependencies for a job, you are using the GitHub cache action. Which input parameters are required? (Each correct answer presents part of the solution. Choose two.)

options.text: path: the file path on the runner to cache or restore
dependency: the name and version of a package to cache or restore
key: the key created when saving a cache and the key used to search for a cache
restore-keys: the copy action key used with cache parameter to cache the data
cache-hit: the copy action key used with restore parameter to restore the data from the cache
ref: the ref name of the branch to access and restore a cache created

questions.explanation: 'path' specifies the filesystem location to cache (A), and 'key' is the unique identifier used to save and retrieve the cache (C). These are the only two required inputs for the actions/cache action.

questions.text: As a developer, how can you identify a composite action on GitHub?

options.text: The action's repository name includes the keyword "composite."
The action's repository includes an init.sh file in the root directory.
The action's repository includes Dockerfile and package.json files.
The action.yml metadata file has the runs.using value set to composite.

questions.explanation: Composite actions are identified by runs.using: 'composite' in the action.yml file. A Dockerfile indicates a Docker action; package.json suggests a JavaScript action.

questions.text: What can be used to set a failed status of an action from its code?

options.text: JavaScript dist/ folder
composite run step
output variable
a non-zero exit code
Dockerfile CMD
@actions/github toolkit

questions.explanation: A non-zero exit code signals failure to GitHub Actions — exit code 0 means success, any other value indicates failure. This is the fundamental convention for reporting action failure.

questions.text: As a developer, you need to use GitHub Actions to deploy a microservice that requires runtime access to a secure token used by multiple teams in different repos. To minimize management overhead and ensure security, which mechanisms should you use to store and access the token? (Each correct answer presents a complete solution. Choose two.)

options.text: Store the token as a GitHub encrypted secret in the same repo. During deployment, use GitHub Actions to store the secret in an environment variable.
Use a corporate non-GitHub secret store (e.g., HashiCorp Vault) to store the token. During deployment, use GitHub Actions to store the secret in an environment variable.
Store the token as an organizational-level encrypted secret in GitHub. During deployment, use GitHub Actions to store the secret in an environment variable.
Store the token as a GitHub encrypted secret in the same repo. Create a reusable custom GitHub Action to access the token by the microservice at runtime.
Store the token in a configuration file in a private repository. Use GitHub Actions to deploy the configuration file to the runtime environment.

questions.explanation: Enterprise-grade secret stores like HashiCorp Vault (B) provide centralized, secure management. Organization-level secrets (C) allow sharing across multiple repositories while maintaining strong encryption and access control.

questions.text: Which of the following statements are true regarding the use of GitHub Actions on a GitHub Enterprise Server instance? (Each correct answer presents a complete solution. Choose three.)

options.text: Actions created by GitHub are automatically available and cannot be disabled.
Third-party actions can be used on GitHub Enterprise Server by configuring GitHub Connect.
Most GitHub-authored actions are automatically bundled for use on GitHub Enterprise Server.
Use of GitHub Actions on GitHub Enterprise Server requires a persistent internet connection.
Actions must be defined in the .github repository.
Third-party actions can be manually synchronized for use on GitHub Enterprise Server.

questions.explanation: GitHub Connect enables access to GitHub.com actions from GHES (B). Most official GitHub actions are pre-bundled with GHES (C). Air-gapped instances can manually sync third-party actions (F). GHES does not require persistent internet.

questions.text:
In the following workflow file, line 5 interprets lines 3 and 4 as Python. Which of the following is a valid option to complete line 5?

1 steps:
2 - run: |
3   import os
4   print(os.environ['PATH'])
5

options.text: shell: python
working-directory: .github/python
shell: bash
with: python

questions.explanation: 'shell: python' tells the runner to pass the run block contents to the Python interpreter. 'shell: bash' would cause the Python code to fail as shell commands. 'with' is for action input parameters, not shell selection.

questions.text: As a developer, you need to make sure that only actions from trusted sources are available for use in your GitHub Enterprise Cloud organization. Which of the following statements are true? (Each correct answer presents a complete solution. Choose three.)

options.text: Actions can be published to an internal marketplace.
GitHub-verified actions can be collectively enabled for use in the enterprise.
Specific actions can individually be enabled for the organization, including version information.
Actions can be restricted to only those available in the enterprise.
Individual third-party actions enabled with a specific tag will prevent updated versions of the action from introducing vulnerabilities.
Actions created by GitHub are automatically enabled and cannot be disabled.

questions.explanation: Enterprises can collectively enable GitHub-verified actions (B), whitelist specific actions with version info (C), and restrict to enterprise-only actions (D). A specific tag does not guarantee vulnerability-free code (E is false).

questions.text: As a developer, you created a JavaScript action. What is the best way to test your JavaScript action?

options.text: Create a workflow that only executes your specific JavaScript action.
Package your JavaScript action inside a docker container image and run it.
Use a tool called @vercel/ncc to compile your code.
Create a workflow that includes the actions/debug-javascript action.

questions.explanation: The actions/debug-javascript action enables Node.js debugging inside the runner via an SSH tunnel, allowing you to set breakpoints, inspect variables, and step through execution.

questions.text: When reviewing an action for use, what file defines its available inputs and outputs?

options.text: inputs.yml
defaults.json
workflow.yml
config.json
action.yml

questions.explanation: Per the exam answer key: workflow.yml. Note: The actual GitHub documentation specifies action.yml as the file that defines an action's inputs and outputs.

questions.text: Which files are required for a Docker container action in addition to the source code? (Each correct answer presents a partial solution. Choose two.)

options.text: action.yml
Actionfile
metadata.yml
Dockerfile

questions.explanation: action.yml is required for all custom actions — it defines metadata, inputs, outputs, and tells GitHub how to run the action (A). The Dockerfile is required specifically for Docker container actions to build the image (D).

questions.text: As a developer, you have configured an IP allow list on a GitHub organization. Which effects does the IP allow list have on GitHub Actions? (Each answer presents a complete solution. Choose two.)

options.text: You can use standard GitHub-hosted runners since their IP addresses are automatically allowed.
You can use self-hosted runners with known IP addresses.
You must allow GitHub Actions's IP address ranges in order to use marketplace actions.
You can use GitHub-hosted larger runners since they can be configured with static IP addresses.

questions.explanation: Standard GitHub-hosted runners are automatically allowed by the IP allow list (A). Self-hosted runners with known IP addresses can be added to the allow list manually (B).

questions.text: You have exactly one Windows x64 self-hosted runner configured with custom tools. Which syntax could you use in the workflow to target that runner?

options.text: runs-on: [self-hosted, windows, x64]
self-hosted: [windows-x64]
runs-on: windows-latest
self-hosted: [windows, x64]

questions.explanation: runs-on accepts an array of labels. 'self-hosted' identifies it as a self-hosted runner, 'windows' and 'x64' are automatically assigned labels based on OS and architecture. Options B and D use invalid syntax — 'self-hosted' is not a top-level keyword.

questions.text: As a developer, which workflow steps should you perform to publish an image to the GitHub Container Registry? (Each correct answer represents part of the solution. Choose three.)

options.text: Push the image to the GitHub Container Registry.
Authenticate to the GitHub Container Registry.
Use the actions/setup-docker action.
Pull the image from the GitHub Container Registry.
Build the container image.

questions.explanation: To publish a container image: first authenticate to the registry (B), then build the image (E), then push it to the registry (A). There is no 'actions/setup-docker' action — Docker is pre-installed on GitHub-hosted runners.

questions.text: While writing a custom action, some behavior within the runner must be changed. Which workflow commands would set an error message in the runner's output? (Each correct answer presents a complete solution. Choose two.)

options.text: echo "::error file=main.py,line=10,col=15::There was an error"
echo "::error=There was an error::"
echo "::error::There was an error"
echo "::error message=There was an error::"

questions.explanation: The error command format is ::error [properties]::message. Option A uses optional file/line/col properties. Option C is the simplest valid form. Option B uses wrong '=' syntax. Option D invents a 'message' parameter that doesn't exist.

questions.text: Which action type should be used to bundle a series of run steps into a reusable custom action?

options.text: Composite action
Bash script action
Docker container action
JavaScript action

questions.explanation: Composite actions are specifically designed to bundle a sequence of shell commands (run steps) into a single reusable action, defined in YAML without requiring Docker or a programming language.

questions.text: Which choices represent best practices for publishing actions so that they can be consumed reliably? (Each correct answer presents a complete solution. Choose two.)

options.text: default branch
commit SHA
repo name
tag
organization name

questions.explanation: A commit SHA (B) is immutable and guarantees the exact version. A tag (D) marks a stable release and should not be moved. Both provide reliable, reproducible references. The default branch can change unexpectedly.

questions.text: What is a valid scenario regarding environment secrets?

options.text: configuring environment secrets for connecting to GitHub Enterprise Server
configuring environment secrets to automatically pull from Azure Key Vault
ensuring a job cannot access environment secrets until approval is obtained from a required reviewer
ensuring only a specific step can access an environment secret

questions.explanation: Using environment secrets to store authentication tokens for accessing a GitHub Enterprise Server from workflows is a valid and realistic scenario. Options B-D describe capabilities that either don't exist natively or are inaccurate.

questions.text: What are two reasons to keep an action in its own repository instead of bundling it with other application code? (Each correct answer presents a complete solution. Choose two.)

options.text: It makes the action.yml file optional.
It makes it easier for the GitHub community to discover the action.
It widens the scope of the code base for developers fixing issues and extending the action.
It decouples the action's versioning from the versioning of other application code.
It allows sharing workflow secrets with other users.

questions.explanation: A separate repository makes the action discoverable on GitHub Marketplace (B) and allows independent versioning and lifecycle management (D), avoiding coupling with application releases.

questions.text: As a developer, you are authoring a workflow that will deploy to both DevCloud and TestCloud resources. Each cloud resource is accessed with a different deployment key. Which approach best allows you to use the same reusable workflow in separate jobs to target the different cloud resources?

options.text: Populate a DEPLOY_KEY repository secret with a JSON object containing DevCloud and TestCloud properties. Then specify DEPLOY_KEY.DevCloud in the secrets sections of the reusable workflow.
Use a marketplace action to conditionally parse the DEPLOY_KEY repository secret based on the cloud resource name.
Store the different keys in a DEPLOY_KEY environment secret in the DevCloud and TestCloud environments. Specify DEPLOY_KEY in the secrets section of the reusable workflow.
Create repository secrets named DevCloud.DEPLOY_KEY and TestCloud.DEPLOY_KEY so that the reusable workflow parses the secrets by resource name.

questions.explanation: A marketplace action can dynamically parse the correct key from DEPLOY_KEY based on a cloud_resource input parameter, keeping the reusable workflow agnostic to specific cloud resources and scalable for future additions.

questions.text: GitHub-hosted runners support which capabilities? (Each correct answer presents a complete solution. Choose two.)

options.text: support for Linux, Windows, and macOS
support for a variety of Linux variations including CentOS, Fedora, and Debian
requiring a payment mechanism (e.g., credit card) to use for private repositories
automatic file-system caching between workflow runs
automatic patching of both the runner and the underlying OS

questions.explanation: GitHub-hosted runners support Linux, Windows, and macOS (A). GitHub automatically patches the runner VMs and their OS (E). Caching is not automatic — it must be configured explicitly with the actions/cache action.

questions.text: An organization's policies specify only local actions are allowed. How should actions be distributed for this organization?

options.text: via a repository owned by a third party
via repositories owned by the organization
via the GitHub Marketplace
via the .github repository owned by the organization

questions.explanation: If only local actions are allowed, all actions must be stored in repositories owned by the organization itself, ensuring security, governance, and compliance with full organizational control.

questions.text: A single secret must be accessed by workflows in specific repositories. What is the best way to create the secret?

options.text: Create an environment secret at the organization level and leverage that environment in each of the specified repositories.
Create an organization secret, specify Selected repositories as the Repository access, and select the required repositories.
Create the secret in one of the repositories, check the Share secret option, and select the required repositories.

Store the secret in a supported external key vault. Configure OpenID Connect (OIDC) to allow access to the external vault and link the secret from the external key vault in each of the specific repositories.

questions.explanation: Creating an environment secret at the organization level and using it in each specified repository offers centralized management with granular control over which repositories can access it.

questions.text: What are the most significant advantages of adding documentation while distributing custom actions? (Each answer presents a complete solution. Choose two.)

options.text: It generates auto completion when using the action in a workflow.
It shares the description of the action to the users.
It provides an example of the action.
It creates a readme.md for the consuming workflow.

questions.explanation: Documentation describes the action's purpose, inputs, and outputs (B), and includes usage examples showing how to reference it in workflows (C), helping users quickly evaluate and adopt the action.

questions.text: A workflow that had been working now stalls in a waiting state until failing. The workflow file process-ml.yaml has not changed and contains jobs specifying runs-on: [gpu]. Which of the following steps would troubleshoot the issue? (Each answer presents a complete solution. Choose two.)

options.text: Review the contents of the Runner_*.log files in the _diag folder.
Increase the usage limits for the GitHub-hosted runners.
Check the "Set up job" step for the logs of the last successful run to determine the runner.
Update the org settings to enable GPU-based GitHub-hosted runners.
Rotate the GITHUB_TOKEN secret for the appropriate runners.

questions.explanation: Runner diagnostic logs in the _diag folder reveal why the runner is offline or failing (A). The 'Set up job' step in previous successful runs shows which runner executed the job and if it's still available (C).

questions.text: Your organization needs to simplify reusing and maintaining automation in your GitHub Enterprise Cloud. Which components can be directly reused across all repositories in an organization? (Each correct answer presents a complete solution. Choose three.)

options.text: actions stored in an organizational partition in the GitHub Marketplace
custom Docker actions stored in GitHub Container Registry
self-hosted runners
encrypted secrets
workflow templates
actions stored in private repositories in the organization

questions.explanation: Self-hosted runners can be configured at the org level (C). Workflow templates provide reusable workflow foundations for all repos (E). Actions in private org repositories can be used by all repos in the same org (F).

questions.text: Which of the following is the best way for an enterprise to prevent certain marketplace actions from running?

options.text: Create a list of the actions that are restricted from being used as an enterprise policy. Every other action can be run.
It is not possible; if an action is in the marketplace, its use cannot be restricted.
Create a list that is maintained as a .yml file in a .github repository specified in the enterprise. Only these actions can be run.
Create a list of the actions that are allowed to run as an enterprise policy. Only these actions can be run.

questions.explanation: An allow-list approach (D) follows the principle of least privilege — only vetted actions can run. A deny-list (A) requires constant maintenance and can miss new malicious actions. GitHub Enterprise does provide action controls.

questions.text: When creating and managing custom actions in an enterprise setting, which of the following is considered a best practice?

options.text: creating a separate repository for each action so that the version can be managed independently
creating a separate branch in application repositories that only contains the actions
creating a single repository for all custom actions so that the versions for each action are all the same
including custom actions that other teams need to reference in the same repository as application code

questions.explanation: A single centralized repository for all custom actions provides discoverability, centralized management, reusability, simplified security audits, and encourages collaboration. Options B and D couple actions to application code.
